questions.question: Why does the SIDH attack NOT apply to CSIDH?

options.text: CSIDH doesn't publish auxiliary torsion points

questions.explanation: The SIDH attack requires auxiliary torsion points φ(P), φ(Q). CSIDH only publishes the codomain curve (j-invariant), with no auxiliary data, so the attack cannot be mounted.

questions.question: What mathematical structure enables CSIDH's key exchange?
questions.explanation: CSIDH uses a commutative class group action on supersingular curves. Because [a]∘[b] = [b]∘[a], Alice and Bob arrive at the same shared curve without auxiliary data.

questions.question: What is CSIDH's main advantage over Kyber?

options.text: Much smaller public keys (64-224 bytes vs 1KB+)

questions.explanation: CSIDH has extremely compact keys (64-224 bytes) compared to Kyber (~1.2KB). This is useful in bandwidth-constrained scenarios, but CSIDH is much slower.

questions.question: What is CSIDH's main disadvantage?

options.text: Very slow operations (100-1000x slower than Kyber)

questions.explanation: CSIDH operations are extremely slow - key generation and exchange take 50-500+ ms versus ~0.1 ms for Kyber. Constant-time implementations are even slower.

questions.question: What is the best known quantum attack against CSIDH?
questions.explanation: Kuperberg's algorithm solves the hidden shift problem underlying CSIDH in subexponential time 2^{O(√log p)}. This is worse than Grover but not polynomial.

questions.question: What type of curves does CSIDH use?

options.text: Supersingular over F_p with special structure

questions.explanation: CSIDH uses supersingular curves over F_p (not F_p²) with a specific structure allowing commutative class group action. The starting curve is typically y² = x³ + x.

questions.question: What does CSIDH publish as a public key?
questions.explanation: CSIDH publishes only the codomain curve (represented by its j-invariant or Montgomery coefficient). No auxiliary points are needed because the class group action is commutative.

questions.question: CSIDH is currently a NIST standardized post-quantum algorithm.
questions.explanation: False. CSIDH is NOT a NIST standard. The NIST selections are ML-KEM (Kyber), ML-DSA (Dilithium), SLH-DSA (SPHINCS+), and Classic McEliece. CSIDH remains a research topic.
